retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Gross alterations become evident after 6 to 12 hours. Gross pathology reveals evidence of transmural infarction or myocardial necrosis in most cases of occlusion myocardial infarction (OMI). However, within hours of infarction, histo-chemically, the necrotic area appears pale with triphenyl tetrazolium chloride, indicating a lack of staining, whereas the uninfarcted area appears brick red. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, cell lysis and enzymatic leakage are used to assess a potential pathology in patients. When a cardiac cell is damaged and then results in cell lysis, cell-specific enzymes are leaked into the extracellular space. The presence of these cell-specific enzymes, troponin, and CK-MB (creatine kinase-muscle/brain), can be used to diagnose myocardial infarctions.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Heart -- Histochemistry and Cytochemistry
retrieved_docs.content: Studying cells and tissue (histochemistry) and intracellular activities (cytochemistry) is useful for narrowing down the correct diagnosis. Immunohistochemistry uses antibodies to target specific antigens in a specimen. The antibody-antigen complex can then be stained to appreciate the presence of the particular antigen. This test can aid in the diagnosis of acute allograft rejection, amyloidosis, neoplasms, and cardiomyopathy. [5]  T-lymphocytes, seen in myocarditis, can also be identified with the help of immunohistochemistry. Immunofluorescence is very similar to immunohistochemistry. However, the antibodies contain a fluorescent dye, which is visible when the antibody is attached to an antigen. Immunofluorescence can assist with the diagnosis of allograft rejection and certain cardiomyopathies. [7] Special stains highlight specific components in a specimen that might be difficult to visualize using hematoxylin and eosin . The Congo red and methyl violet stains are useful for detecting amyloid deposits in tissue. In myocarditis or allograft rejection, methyl green-pyronine stain can spot lymphocytes. Masson's elastic trichrome stains connective tissue, such as elastic fiber and collagen. In patients with iron-overload cardiomyopathy, possibly due to hemochromatosis, any iron deposition in the tissue can be stained using the Prussian blue stain. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Heart -- Tissue Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Histological and cytological studies of the heart are necessary for diagnostic purposes, assessment of allograft rejection after a cardiac transplant, or evaluation of the effect of drug toxicity on the heart. An endomyocardial biopsy obtains cardiac tissue to be analyzed. During an endomyocardial biopsy, 1 to 2 mm3 of endocardium and myocardium are taken from the right ventricle. [5] Peripheral proximity to the venous entry of the bioptome and a thicker wall relative to the atrium make the right ventricle an ideal location for a biopsy. The cardiac sample is then placed into a fixative, such as formalin, to preserve the tissue. These preserved samples are placed into cassettes, embedded in paraffin wax, thinly sliced, and mounted onto glass slides. Hematoxylin and eosin are initial, basic stains for visualization of the heart tissue under light microscopy. Depending on the purpose of the endomyocardial biopsy, the method to prepare the tissue may vary. For instance, if viral myocarditis is on the differential, a frozen sample is needed to identify the virus through a polymerase chain reaction. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The gross pathological, histochemical, light microscopic, and ultrastructural findings vary with coronary occlusion and ischemia onset.

retrieved_docs.title: Rodenticide Toxicity -- Evaluation -- Troponin
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated troponin levels may signify cardiac myocyte damage caused by zinc and aluminum phosphide.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation -- Radionuclide Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Technetium 99m Sestamibi SPECT: Tc-99m Sestamibi is a cation that diffuses across the cell membrane and then into the mitochondria, and positive uptake is suggestive of viable myocardial cells. The protocols used are rest-stress and rest protocol with nitrate enhancement to promote uptake. [16] Normal uptake is consistent with viable myocardium, moderate uptake is consistent with a mixture of infarct and viable tissue, and absent uptake is consistent with an infarct.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The physiology of the ischemic cascade is fundamental for a stress test and myocardial viability assessment, as different imaging modalities utilize different stages of the cascade. The following imaging modalities are commonly used in the evaluation of myocardial viability.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Heart -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: A large part of the cardiac wall is made up of the myocardium. Cardiomyocytes join together to make up this layer. These cardiomyocytes are striated like myocytes found in skeletal muscle. However, unlike skeletal muscle cells, they are branched, contain intercalated disks, and are usually mononucleated. They are also unable to regenerate. After an insult, such as a myocardial infarction, the necrotic area gets replaced by scar tissue. This histological change is evident under light microscopy as the fibrous component of the scar tissue is stained blue with Masson's elastic trichrome stain. The endocardium comprises a single layer of endothelial cells lining the heart chambers. Occasionally, small amounts of smooth muscle can also be in the endocardium. Compared to the right atrium, the left atrium has a thicker endocardium because of high pressure from the pulmonary veins. The subendothelial layer, between the myocardium and endocardium, contains loose elastic tissue, collagen bundles, nerves, and occasionally blood vessels.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Healing continues and may be complete as early as 4 weeks or require 8 weeks or longer, depending on the extent of necrosis. Occasionally, larger infarcts may heal at the borders, leaving the central area with mummified myocytes for extended periods.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation -- Radionuclide Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotracers used in the viability study are the analogs of our endogenous second messenger system and metabolites used in cell signaling and metabolism. Quantitative analysis of these radiotracers' uptake thus yields indirect evidence of intact cellular function and the cell membrane and subcellular organelles' integrity. These tracers have unstable nuclei and electrons, which emit characteristic radiation after being taken up by the myocardial cells, which are converted to photons and imaged by a camera system.

retrieved_docs.title: Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy  -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Endomyocardial biopsy of patients with takotsubo cardiomyopathy demonstrates reversible focal lysis of myocytes, mononuclear infiltrates, and contraction band necrosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Stunned Myocardium (Archived) -- Evaluation -- Transthoracic Echocardiography (TTE)
retrieved_docs.content: Myocardial necrosis is associated with myocardial thinning; preserved end-diastolic wall thickness (EDWT) may provide a simple index of myocardial viability that can be assessed with a resting echocardiogram. EDWT less than or equal to 0.6 cm exclude myocardial viability. [20]

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Stunned Myocardium (Archived) -- Evaluation -- Single-photon Emission Computed Tomography (SPECT)
retrieved_docs.content: The most commonly used tracers for SPECT include thallium-201 (Tl) and technetium-99m (Tc) –labeled compounds. Myocardial uptake of Tl 4 to 24 hours after injection reflects the integrity of sarcolemma, revealing tissue viability. [22] Technetium-99m (Tc) labeled compounds are lipophilic molecules. Myocardial uptake of these compounds can provide information on both myocardial perfusion and viability. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Endomyocardial Biopsy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: To reduce discomfort during the procedure, analgesia and sedation are necessary. The biopsy procedure takes several minutes on average. To reduce sampling error, several tissue samples should be taken. The preparation of histologic samples begins just after the biopsy sample is taken. The different tissue samples are stored in formaldehyde, glutaraldehyde, and liquid nitrogen for evaluation using light microscopy, electron microscopy, immunofluorescence, or viral nucleic acid studies. For histologic examination, the samples are stained using hematoxylin-eosin, Masson trichrome, Congo red for identifying amyloidosis, and Prussian blue to detect iron deposition. The following characteristic changes are identified: [13]

Recognizing that these diverse clinical presentations share the same myocardial and conduction‑system substrate and acute infarct morphology is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely therapeutic intervention, and preservation of both contractile function and electrical stability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 311
retrieved_docs.content: Shock, a clinical syndrome of inadequate tissue perfusion, is classified as صَدْمَة قَلْبِيَّة المَنْشَأ, which denotes cardiogenic shock—a form of shock that originates from primary cardiac dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are clinical situations in which the use of digoxin is contraindicated, meaning that digoxin should not be administered to patients experiencing these life‑threatening cardiac arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2819
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ) is closely related and may be considered synonymous with coronary artery occlusion (انسداد الشريان التاجي). It is also mapped to coronary artery embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي), indicating that these terms describe similar pathological events in which a blockage of a coronary artery—whether by occlusion or embolic material—leads to sudden injury of the heart muscle. Recognizing the interchangeability of these concepts assists clinicians in diagnosing and managing the acute coronary syndrome that results from compromised coronary blood flow.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2843
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are clinical situations in which the use of digitoxin is contraindicated; digitoxin should not be administered to patients experiencing these life‑threatening arrhythmias because it can exacerbate cardiac instability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 975
retrieved_docs.content: artery, intermediate artery). Despite these variations in presentation and vascular involvement, the pathological finding site for all of them is the myocardial tissue, underscoring that myocardial injury is the central feature of acute coronary syndromes regardless of the precise ECG morphology or culprit vessel. Recognizing the specific type of infarction and its associated arterial occlusion aids clinicians in tailoring reperfusion strategies and optimizing patient care while acknowledging that the underlying myocardial damage remains the pivotal concern.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2624
retrieved_docs.content: proximal, mid, distal, intermediate, posterior‑lateral branch, left anterior descending), each condition reflects acute injury to myocardial tissue. Recognizing that the myocardium is the universal finding site across these varied presentations aids clinicians in correlating ECG findings, angiographic territories, and clinical contexts to guide diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 576
retrieved_docs.content: further emphasizes the importance of recognizing valvular pathology when evaluating and managing cardiac patients. Collectively, the interconnections among acute myocardial infarction, various forms of heart muscle injury, coronary arterial thrombotic events, associated arrhythmias, and related systemic conditions illustrate the need for integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in cardiovascular medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2399
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations—including ST‑segment elevation infarctions caused by occlusion of the left anterior descending artery, its septal or proximal branches, the intermediate artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending or posterior lateral branches of the right coronary artery, as well as non‑ST‑segment elevation infarctions, widespread infarctions, and those occurring during procedures or postoperatively—always involves injury to the myocardium. Regardless of the specific coronary vessel or segment that is blocked, the pathological finding site for each of these myocardial infarction subtypes is the myocardial tissue itself, underscoring that the heart muscle is the central organ affected across the full spectrum of acute infarct presentations. This unified understanding emphasizes that diagnostic evaluation, therapeutic interventions, and patient monitoring should focus on the extent and location of myocardial damage, irrespective of the particular coronary artery involved or the clinical context of the infarction.

retrieved_docs.content: valve pathologies, broader cardiac disease categories, diagnostic modalities and therapeutic interventions is essential for accurate diagnosis, optimal treatment planning and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2631
retrieved_docs.content: Organic nitrates used in cardiac disease constitute a class of vasodilators employed in the treatment of cardiac conditions, and isosorbide mononitrate is a member of this organic nitrate class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined heart disease is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب, a term that describes dilation of the blood vessels within the heart. This equivalence indicates that the ambiguous classification of certain cardiac conditions corresponds to a pathological enlargement of the coronary or myocardial vasculature, which can affect cardiac function and warrants appropriate diagnostic evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2667
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure described as an operation on the heart and pericardium represents a broad category of cardiac and pericardial surgeries, within which the specific technique known as خزعة التأمور (pericardial biopsy) is included. This relationship indicates that a pericardial biopsy is a particular type of intervention that falls under the larger umbrella of operations targeting both the heart muscle and its surrounding sac, highlighting its role as a focused diagnostic or therapeutic maneuver within comprehensive cardiac surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2292
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedure on heart, NOS is classified as a type of procedure on the heart, indicating that it falls within the broader category of cardiac procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: procedure on heart, NOS” are organized, the latter being an inverse ISA of “Procedure on heart” to emphasize its diagnostic nature. Diagnostic procedures on the heart and pericardium are also placed under “diagnostic cardiac catheterization / coronary arteriography” and “other diagnostic cardiovascular procedures,” reflecting their role in invasive cardiac imaging. The therapeutic branch “HEART: EXCISIONS” is a subset of heart biopsy and comprises excisions such as excision of a heart valve. Heart biopsy (خزبة القلب) itself is a type of “Procedure on heart” and branches into open left ventricular biopsy, open right ventricular biopsy, and transluminal left ventricular biopsy; the open ventricular biopsies are simultaneously classified as forms of open‑heart surgery, linking biopsy techniques to major cardiac surgical interventions. Procedures classified as “Other OR heart procedures” fall under “Other operations on heart and pericardium” and are associated with the papillary muscles

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2660
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on heart and pericardium are classified as a subset of other diagnostic cardiovascular procedures, indicating that they belong to the broader group of cardiovascular diagnostic tests. Within this category, there is a further subdivision called Other diagnostic procedures on heart and pericardium, which represents a more specific group of tests focused on the heart and its surrounding pericardial sac. This hierarchical organization clarifies how heart‑ and pericardium‑specific diagnostics fit into the overall framework of cardiovascular diagnostic testing, aiding clinicians in selecting and categorizing appropriate investigative procedures for cardiac and pericardial conditions.
